planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring as fronds begin to emerge; keep soil consistently moist until established.

directions: Plant in deep to partial shade in humus-rich, consistently moist soil with good drainage, preferably in a limestone-influenced substrate to mimic its native cliff-face habitat. Set divisions at the same depth they were previously growing, spacing them 12 to 18 inches apart to allow room for the colony to expand. The small green bulblets that form along the midrib of fronds will drop naturally and root where they land, so expect gradual self-colonization in hospitable spots. Mulch lightly with leaf mold each autumn to protect shallow roots and maintain the cool, moisture-retentive conditions this fern requires.

planting.note: Divide and transplant in early spring before fronds fully unfurl; water thoroughly after planting.

directions: Choose a shaded location with rich, moist soil such as a woodland garden floor or the base of a slope where moisture collects naturally. Plant divisions in spring at the same depth they were previously growing, and water generously through the first growing season. Do not be alarmed if fronds yellow and die back during dry summer spells; this species is summer-deciduous in drier conditions and will reliably re-emerge in fall with cooler temperatures and increased moisture. Supplement with compost or leaf litter each year to maintain the fertile, woodland-soil conditions this fern prefers.

planting.note: Plant small divisions in early spring into crevices or well-drained rocky soil; avoid wet or clay-heavy sites.

directions: Grow in a partly shaded rock garden or on a retaining wall with excellent drainage and a gritty, low-nutrient substrate; avoid rich soils which can cause overly lush growth prone to collapse. Tuck small divisions into crevices filled with a mix of grit and coarse humus, mimicking the alpine outcrops where this fern thrives naturally. Water sparingly once established, as this species is notably drought-tolerant once its roots have anchored into the rock substrate. The distinctive rust-colored hairs on the frond undersides intensify in sunnier exposures, making a partly open site preferable to deep shade.

planting.note: Divide clumps in early spring or autumn; plant at the same depth and water in well.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in any reasonably moist, well-drained soil, where it will form tidy, slowly spreading clumps of hairy, grass-like foliage. This woodrush tolerates a wider range of conditions than many of its relatives, including brief dry spells and more heat, making it a practical groundcover under deciduous trees. Divide established clumps every three to four years to maintain vigor and to propagate new plants; divisions establish readily when kept moist for the first few weeks. The loose white flower panicles that appear in early summer add a delicate, airy texture to the shade garden before fading to tan seed heads.

planting.note: Sow seed directly in autumn under deciduous trees; cold stratification over winter improves germination rates.

directions: Sow seed in autumn directly onto lightly raked, moist soil beneath deciduous trees, where natural leaf litter will provide cover and cold winter temperatures will stratify the seed for spring germination. This tall woodland grass prefers humus-rich, moist soil and will produce its most graceful nodding spikelets under moderate shade. Once established, it self-seeds freely in favorable conditions, creating naturalistic drifts without becoming invasive; simply remove unwanted seedlings while young if spread needs managing. Avoid fertilizing as excess nitrogen produces overly tall, floppy growth that loses the elegant, arching habit that makes this grass so ornamentally valuable.

planting.note: Direct sow in early autumn or divide established clumps in spring; autumn sowing allows roots to establish before winter.

directions: Direct sow in early autumn onto a lightly prepared seedbed under deciduous trees or in dappled shade, pressing seed into the soil surface without covering deeply, as light aids germination. Unlike most ornamental fescues that demand full sun, this species genuinely performs well in moderate to full shade, maintaining attractive dark-green, thread-fine foliage throughout the season. Plant or thin to 12 to 18 inches apart to allow each clump to develop its full, loose arching character. Divide clumps every three years in early spring to prevent center die-out and to maintain the fresh, dense texture that makes this fescue a standout groundcover in shaded situations.
